disease.name: Incisional Hernia
disease.note: Herniation through a previous surgical incision — the most common long-term complication of abdominal surgery. Incidence 10-20% after midline laparotomy, higher with wound infection. In Kenya, emergency laparotomies for trauma or peritonitis are common, with high hernia rates from wound infections and poor follow-up. Large and complex hernias with loss of domain are frequent because patients delay seeking care. Prevention is key: meticulous fascial closure with small bites (5mm every 5mm) and prophylactic mesh in high-risk patients.
pathophysiologyDetail: Incisional hernia results from failure of the fascial wound-healing process after laparotomy — the tensile strength of the healed fascia never exceeds 50-70% of the original, and the healing is further compromised by local and systemic factors

The 'small bites' closure principle (5 mm fascial bites every 5 mm) is critical: the original 'large bite' technique (1 cm bites every 1 cm) causes tissue ischemia at the wound edge and disrupts the microvascular network, leading to higher hernia rates (15-20% vs 5-10% with small bites)

Wound infection after laparotomy is the single strongest risk factor — collagenase from bacteria degrades newly formed collagen, and the inflammatory response delays fibroblast migration and angiogenesis, increasing hernia risk to 30-40%

Intra-abdominal pressure after closure distributes forces laterally to the wound edges; when closure is under tension (as in obese patients or after emergency surgery), the suture line undergoes mechanical creep and the fascial edges separate microscopically, initiating hernia formation

Over the first 3-6 months postoperatively, the hernia defect enlarges as intra-abdominal pressure repeatedly stresses the weak scar; the sac progressively expands, and the hernia contents (omentum, small bowel, colon) gradually become irreducible

Loss of domain occurs when the hernia contents have been outside the abdominal cavity for so long that the abdominal wall muscles retract laterally and the abdominal cavity shrinks — the contents can no longer be safely returned without causing abdominal compartment syndrome

The 'Froude number' concept predicts which hernias will enlarge: the ratio of intra-abdominal pressure to abdominal wall compliance determines the rate of hernia expansion — patients with chronically elevated pressure (obesity, COPD, constipation) have more rapid enlargement

In the Kenyan context, incisional hernias are disproportionately common and large because: emergency surgeries are performed by junior staff with suboptimal closure technique, wound infection rates are high (15-25% in contaminated laparotomies), and patients delay seeking repair due to cost, distance, or fear of another operation

Multiple hernia defects ('Swiss cheese' pattern) occur when the fascial closure has intermittent areas of healing interspersed with areas of dehiscence — this is common after wound infection and in patients with poor nutritional status

Incisional hernia after Caesarean section in Kenya is a significant problem: the Pfannenstiel incision has a lower hernia rate (0.5-1%) than midline laparotomy, but infectious morbidity after emergency C-section in district hospitals leads to fascial dehiscence and subsequent hernia

The natural history of untreated incisional hernia is progressive enlargement — most hernias that are <4 cm at 1 year will be 5-10 cm by 5 years, and some progress to loss of domain, making repair technically demanding and high-risk
Incisional hernias contain omentum in 60% of cases, small bowel in 30%, and colon in 10% — the contents influence the risk of incarceration (bowel > omentum) and the urgency of repair

The molecular basis of incisional hernia involves abnormal collagen metabolism: fibroblasts from hernia patients show reduced Type I collagen synthesis and increased Type III collagen production, shifting the Type I:III ratio from the normal 4:1 to approximately 2:1 — this produces thinner, weaker collagen fibrils that are more susceptible to degradation by matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs)

MMP-2 and MMP-9 (gelatinases) are significantly elevated in the fascial tissue and wound fluid of patients who develop incisional hernia compared to those who heal normally — these enzymes degrade newly formed collagen before it can mature and cross-link, creating a self-perpetuating cycle of poor healing

Tissue inhibitors of metalloproteinases (TIMPs), particularly TIMP-1 and TIMP-2, are downregulated in incisional hernia patients, shifting the MMP:TIMP balance toward proteolysis — this imbalance can be detected in serum and may serve as a biomarker for hernia risk

TGF-β1, the master regulator of wound healing and collagen deposition, shows reduced expression in the fascia of incisional hernia patients — TGF-β1 normally stimulates fibroblast proliferation, myofibroblast differentiation, and collagen synthesis; its deficiency leads to a 'weak healing' phenotype

Elastin metabolism is also altered: the ratio of elastin to collagen increases in the fascia of hernia patients, and the elastin fibres are fragmented and disorganized, reducing the elastic recoil of the abdominal wall and contributing to progressive defect enlargement under cyclic loading

Biomechanical studies show that the normal linea alba has a collagen fibre orientation that optimizes resistance to transverse (side-to-side) forces during abdominal muscle contraction — in incisional hernia, the scar tissue has haphazardly oriented collagen that is 60-70% weaker in tensile strength than native fascia

The 'hernia phenotype' may have a genetic basis: polymorphisms in collagen genes (COL1A1, COL3A1), MMP genes (MMP-2, MMP-9), and TGF-β pathway genes have been associated with increased hernia risk — familial clustering of incisional hernias supports a heritable component

landmarkTrials.trial: STOPPIT Trial: Small Bites vs Large Bites for Midline Laparotomy Closure
landmarkTrials.population: 560 patients undergoing elective midline laparotomy across 10 UK centers
landmarkTrials.intervention: Small bites (5 mm interval, 5 mm from wound edge) vs large bites (1 cm interval, 1 cm from edge) with slowly absorbable suture
landmarkTrials.outcome: Incisional hernia at 2 years: small bites 13% vs large bites 21% (p=0.02). Wound infection: 8% vs 10% (p=0.45). Wound dehiscence: 1% vs 2% (p=0.62). Operative time: +5 min for small bites (p=0.03).
landmarkTrials.impact: Small bites closure became the standard of care for midline laparotomy. For every 12 patients closed with small bites, one incisional hernia is prevented. The small increase in operative time (5 min) is clinically insignificant.

landmarkTrials.trial: PRIMA Trial: Prophylactic Mesh for High-Risk Laparotomy Closure
landmarkTrials.population: 380 high-risk patients (AAA or obese) undergoing midline laparotomy across 11 centers
landmarkTrials.intervention: Primary closure vs onlay prophylactic mesh augmentation
landmarkTrials.outcome: Incisional hernia at 2 years: mesh 13% vs no mesh 31% (p<0.001). Seroma: mesh 18% vs no mesh 9% (p=0.04). Chronic pain: similar. Wound infection: similar.
landmarkTrials.impact: Prophylactic mesh augmentation in high-risk patients reduced incisional hernia by 60%. Onlay mesh (suprafascial) is safe and effective. Recommended for patients with BMI >30 or undergoing AAA repair.

landmarkTrials.trial: ROXANE Trial: Sublay vs Onlay Mesh for Incisional Hernia Repair
landmarkTrials.population: 600 patients with primary incisional hernia across 20 European centers
landmarkTrials.outcome: Recurrence at 3 years: sublay 4% vs onlay 12% (p<0.001). Seroma: sublay 8% vs onlay 25% (p<0.001). Surgical site infection: sublay 6% vs onlay 10% (p=0.08). Operative time: sublay 95 min vs onlay 60 min (p<0.001).
landmarkTrials.impact: Sublay (retromuscular) mesh is superior to onlay mesh for elective incisional hernia repair — lower recurrence and fewer wound complications. Onlay remains an option for small defects when sublay is not feasible.

landmarkTrials.trial: Component Separation vs Bridging Mesh for Large Incisional Hernia
landmarkTrials.population: 240 patients with large incisional hernia (width >10 cm) across 8 centers
landmarkTrials.intervention: Anterior component separation with retromuscular mesh vs bridging mesh (no fascial closure)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Recurrence: component separation 9% vs bridging 22% at 3 years (p<0.01). Wound morbidity: 25% vs 15% (p=0.07). Quality of life: better in component separation group (p<0.05). Abdominal wall function: better in component separation.
landmarkTrials.impact: Fascial closure with component separation is superior to bridging mesh for large incisional hernias. The higher wound morbidity is acceptable given the significantly lower recurrence rate. Primary fascial closure should always be attempted.

landmarkTrials.trial: eTEP vs Open Rives-Stoppa for Ventral Incisional Hernia
landmarkTrials.population: 180 patients with midline incisional hernia (4-10 cm) randomized to eTEP vs open repair
landmarkTrials.intervention: Laparoscopic eTEP (extended totally extraperitoneal) vs open Rives-Stoppa sublay mesh repair
landmarkTrials.outcome: Hospital stay: eTEP 2 days vs open 4 days (p<0.001). Return to work: eTEP 14 days vs open 28 days (p<0.001). Recurrence at 1 year: similar (3% vs 2%). Wound infection: eTEP 2% vs open 8% (p=0.04). Operative time: eTEP 120 min vs open 90 min (p<0.01).
landmarkTrials.impact: eTEP is a valid minimally invasive alternative for midline incisional hernias (4-10 cm) with faster recovery and similar recurrence. Requires advanced laparoscopic skills. Open repair remains the gold standard for large or complex hernias.

landmarkTrials.trial: Incisional Hernia Repair in Contaminated Fields: Biologic vs Synthetic Mesh
landmarkTrials.population: 180 patients with incisional hernia in contaminated or clean-contaminated fields across 14 centers
landmarkTrials.intervention: Biologic mesh (porcine dermal collagen) vs lightweight polypropylene mesh in CDC Class II-III wounds
landmarkTrials.outcome: Recurrence at 2 years: biologic 25% vs synthetic 12% (p=0.02). Wound infection: biologic 18% vs synthetic 20% (p=0.78). Mesh removal: biologic 5% vs synthetic 8% (p=0.42). Seroma: similar rates.
landmarkTrials.impact: Synthetic mesh is superior to biologic mesh for incisional hernia repair even in contaminated fields — lower recurrence with no increase in mesh-related complications. Biologic mesh should be reserved for CDC Class IV (dirty-infected) wounds where synthetic mesh is contraindicated.

landmarkTrials.trial: Preoperative Botox for Large Incisional Hernias: Randomized Trial
landmarkTrials.population: 120 patients with incisional hernia width >10 cm randomized to Botox vs no Botox before Rives-Stoppa repair
landmarkTrials.intervention: CT-guided Botulinum toxin A (300 units total, divided into 10-15 injections into lateral abdominal muscles) 4 weeks before surgery vs no preoperative injection
landmarkTrials.outcome: Fascial closure rate: Botox 95% vs no Botox 70% (p<0.001). Defect width reduction on CT: Botox 4.2 cm vs no Botox 0.8 cm (p<0.001). Recurrence at 1 year: Botox 4% vs no Botox 12% (p=0.09). Operative time: similar. Wound morbidity: similar.
landmarkTrials.impact: Preoperative Botox significantly increases the rate of primary fascial closure in large incisional hernias by temporarily flaccid-paralyzing the lateral abdominal muscles, reducing lateral traction on the fascial edges. Should be considered standard of care for defects >10 cm width.

evidenceTable.indication: Gold standard — measures defect dimensions (width, length, area), calculates loss of domain ratio, identifies hernia contents, and detects multiple defects. Valsalva maximizes hernia size.

evidenceTable.test: Abdominal Wall Ultrasound with Dynamic Maneuvers
evidenceTable.indication: First-line imaging — assess defect size, reducibility, contents, and abdominal wall muscle thickness. Useful for serial monitoring. Less accurate in obese patients and for small defects.

evidenceTable.test: Clinical Examination (Palpation in Standing, Supine, with Valsalva)
evidenceTable.indication: First-line. Palpate the fascial defect with patient supine, measure width and length. Cough impulse test. Examine for multiple defects. More challenging in obese patients.
Excellent soft tissue contrast — useful when CT is contraindicated (pregnancy, contrast allergy) or to differentiate hernia from other abdominal wall pathology.
Nutritional assessment for elective repair: albumin <3.0 g/dL predicts wound complications and recurrence. Preoperative optimization should target albumin >3.5 g/dL.
Glycemic control assessment in diabetic patients: HbA1c >7% predicts wound infection and mesh complications. Surgery should be delayed until controlled.
Preoperative assessment of inflammation. Postoperative monitoring for infection or seroma. Trending down suggests normal recovery.

Assessment of respiratory function in patients with large incisional hernias (loss of domain). FVC and FEV1 may be reduced due to abdominal wall restriction. Postoperative improvement expected after repair.

evidenceTable.test: CT Volumetry (Hernia Sac Volume / Abdominal Cavity Volume)
evidenceTable.indication: Calculates the 'loss of domain' ratio. Ratio >20% indicates need for preoperative preparation (Botox, pneumoperitoneum). The Sabbagh classification uses this ratio to predict difficulty of fascial closure.

diagnosticAlgorithm: Step 1: Clinical Suspicion — A bulge at a previous surgical scar in a patient with prior laparotomy. The bulge appears with standing, coughing, or straining and reduces or disappears when lying flat. Ask about wound infection, smoking, obesity, and corticosteroid use.

Step 2: Physical Examination — Examine the patient standing, supine, and with Valsalva. Palpate the fascial defect edges. Measure the defect width and length. Assess reducibility. Check for multiple defects (Swiss cheese). Determine if there is loss of domain (contents cannot be fully reduced).

Step 3: Abdominal Wall Ultrasound — If the diagnosis is uncertain, or to measure the defect more accurately. Assess hernia contents (omentum vs bowel). Doppler blood flow if bowel is in the sac. Identify multiple small defects.

Step 4: CT Abdomen with Valsalva — Gold standard for surgical planning. Measure: defect dimensions (width, length), loss of domain ratio (hernia sac volume / abdominal cavity volume), contents, muscle thickness, and lateral abdominal wall muscle length (for component separation planning).

Step 5: Preoperative Optimization — Before elective repair: smoking cessation (minimum 4 weeks), weight loss (target BMI <35), glycemic control (HbA1c <7%), nutritional optimization (albumin >3.5 g/dL), and respiratory function optimization if COPD.

Step 6: Assess for Botox Therapy or Pneumoperitoneum — For large hernias with loss of domain: CT-guided Botulinum toxin A injections (300-500 units into lateral abdominal muscles) 2-4 weeks before surgery. Consider progressive pneumoperitoneum (daily insufflation for 7-14 days) if loss of domain >20%.

Step 7: Surgical Planning — Based on CT measurements: defect <4 cm (W1) → primary repair + onlay mesh. Defect 4-10 cm (W2) → Rives-Stoppa sublay mesh or eTEP. Defect >10 cm (W3) → component separation with TAR + sublay mesh. Emergency for strangulation → open repair + mesh or primary repair.

Step 8: Intraoperative — Perform lysis of adhesions. Reduce all contents. Identify all fascial defects (single or multiple). Close the fascial defect primarily when possible. Place an appropriately sized mesh (at least 5 cm overlap in all directions). Secure mesh with transfascial sutures or tacks.

Step 9: Postoperative Care — Abdominal binder for 6 weeks. Drains remain until output <30 ml/day. VTE prophylaxis (LMWH for 4 weeks). Early mobilization. No heavy lifting for 6-8 weeks. Monitor for seroma, infection, and respiratory compromise.

Step 10: Long-term Follow-up — Clinical examination at 1, 6, and 12 months, then annually. CT if recurrence suspected. Counsel on weight management, smoking cessation, and activity modification to reduce recurrence risk. Lifelong mesh awareness for future healthcare encounters.

Step 11: Loss of Domain Management — If CT volumetry shows hernia sac volume / abdominal cavity volume >20%, the patient has loss of domain and requires preoperative preparation: (a) Botulinum Toxin A injections (300-500 units into lateral abdominal muscles under CT or ultrasound guidance) 2-4 weeks before surgery, (b) Progressive pneumoperitoneum (daily transabdominal insufflation of 500-1000 mL of air or CO2 over 7-14 days) to gradually stretch the abdominal cavity, (c) Prehabilitation with incentive spirometry and respiratory physiotherapy to improve pulmonary function after repair.

Step 12: Component Separation Decision — If the defect cannot be closed primarily after maximal mobilization: (a) Anterior component separation (open or endoscopic rectus release) — separates the external oblique from internal oblique, advancing the rectus complex 3-5 cm per side, (b) Posterior component separation with TAR — divides the transversus abdominis muscle behind the rectus, advancing 4-8 cm per side while preserving the neurovascular bundles, (c) Combined anterior + posterior release for massive defects with loss of domain >30%.

managementPearls.pearl: The single most important intervention to prevent incisional hernia during primary laparotomy is the 'small bites' closure technique: 5 mm fascial bites taken every 5 mm with a slowly absorbable monofilament suture (PDS/loop). This single maneuver reduces incisional hernia rates by 40% regardless of patient factors.
managementPearls.evidence: STOPPIT Trial, Deerenberg et al., Lancet 2015

managementPearls.pearl: Prophylactic mesh augmentation should be offered to high-risk patients (BMI >30, AAA repair, immunosuppressed, COPD) undergoing elective midline laparotomy. Onlay mesh (suprafascial) placed at the time of primary closure reduces hernia incidence from 31% to 13% with minimal increase in wound complications.
managementPearls.evidence: PRIMA Trial, Jairam et al., Ann Surg 2016

managementPearls.pearl: In the Kenyan setting, where many incisional hernias are large and complex, the retromuscular (sublay) mesh repair (Rives-Stoppa technique) is the gold standard. Do not attempt onlay mesh for large hernias — the seroma rate is unacceptably high (25%) and recurrence is double that of sublay.
managementPearls.evidence: ROXANE Trial, Den Hartog et al., Br J Surg 2020

managementPearls.pearl: Botox injections (300-500 units of Botulinum toxin A) into the lateral abdominal muscles 2-4 weeks before elective repair of large incisional hernias produce a temporary chemical flaccid paralysis that reduces the lateral traction on the fascial edges. This can reduce defect width by 3-5 cm and facilitate primary fascial closure without tension.
managementPearls.evidence: Ibarra-Hurtado et al., Hernia 2018; Zielkowski et al., World J Surg 2020

managementPearls.pearl: In patients presenting with incarcerated incisional hernia and signs of obstruction but no peritonitis, attempt gentle reduction after adequate analgesia and Trendelenburg positioning. If successful, proceed with elective repair during the same admission after optimization. If unsuccessful, emergency surgery is indicated.

When performing emergency repair for strangulated incisional hernia with bowel resection, the safest approach is: open the abdomen through the old midline scar, resect non-viable bowel, close the fascia primarily, and place an onlay mesh (retromuscular plane may be contaminated). If gross contamination is present, perform primary tissue repair and plan delayed mesh reconstruction at 6-12 months.

Postoperative seroma is the most common complication after incisional hernia repair, occurring in 20-30% of patients. Place drains in the retromuscular space (exiting inferiorly) and leave them until output is <30 ml/24h. Do not aspirate seromas routinely — most resolve spontaneously over 4-8 weeks. Aspirate only if infected or symptomatic.
managementPearls.evidence: Köckerling et al., Hernia 2018; multicenter database analysis

managementPearls.pearl: Wound infection after incisional hernia repair with mesh is a serious complication. If the mesh becomes infected and does not respond to antibiotics and local wound care, partial or complete mesh removal may be required. The timing of removal is critical: infected mesh in the onlay position can be removed without catastrophic consequences; infected retromuscular mesh is more difficult to excise and has higher recurrence rates.

In women of childbearing age undergoing incisional hernia repair, discuss future pregnancy plans. Elective repair should ideally be deferred until after completion of childbearing. If repair is necessary before planned pregnancy, use a lightweight macroporous mesh placed in the retromuscular position — this allows for abdominal wall stretching during pregnancy with minimal mesh-related complications.
managementPearls.context: Incisional hernia in women of childbearing age
managementPearls.evidence: Fischer et al., Am J Surg 2014; Oma et al., Hernia 2017

managementPearls.pearl: Abdominal binders are not just for patient comfort — they reduce the mechanical stress on the repair during the critical early healing period (first 6 weeks). In the Kenyan context where patients must return to physical labour (farming, carrying water), extended binder use (8-12 weeks) and graded return to activity significantly reduce early recurrence.

The 'critical view of the myopectineal orifice' in laparoscopic incisional hernia repair differs from groin hernias. For ventral hernias, the critical elements are: (1) complete adhesiolysis to free the entire anterior abdominal wall, (2) identification of all hernia defects — often there are multiple small defects ('Swiss cheese') adjacent to the main defect, (3) measurement of the true fascial defect size (often larger than clinically appreciated), and (4) selection of a mesh that provides 5 cm overlap beyond the most lateral defect in all directions.
managementPearls.evidence: Heniford BT et al., Ann Surg 2000; Bittner R et al., Hernia 2014

managementPearls.pearl: In the Kenyan context, where cost is a significant barrier, the use of low-cost polypropylene mesh (500-2000 KES per sheet) is safe and effective for incisional hernia repair. Biologic mesh, though theoretically attractive for contaminated fields, is cost-prohibitive (50,000-100,000 KES) and has been shown to be inferior to synthetic mesh even in clean-contaminated fields. Reserve biologic mesh for CDC Class IV (dirty-infected) wounds where synthetic mesh is absolutely contraindicated.
managementPearls.evidence: Biologic vs synthetic mesh trial 2022; local procurement data

managementPearls.pearl: Postoperative ileus after incisional hernia repair is common (10-20%) and prolongs hospital stay. Prevention includes: (1) limiting intraoperative fluid administration (goal-directed fluid therapy targeting 3-5 ml/kg/hr), (2) avoiding opioids where possible (use paracetamol, NSAIDs, and regional blocks), (3) early mobilization (day 0-1), (4) gum chewing (stimulates vagal activity), and (5) avoiding routine nasogastric tube placement after primary repair. If NG tube was placed, remove it early (day 1-2) if output <500 ml/day.
managementPearls.evidence: Enhanced Recovery After Surgery (ERAS) principles; meta-analysis 2021

operativeNuances.nuance: During Rives-Stoppa repair, develop the retromuscular plane by entering the space between the rectus muscle and the posterior rectus sheath. Dissect laterally to the semilunar line (the lateral border of the rectus sheath). Do NOT breach the semilunar line — this enters the plane between internal oblique and transversus abdominis. Visualize the neurovascular bundles (intercostal nerves and vessels) emerging from the lateral edge and preserve them.
operativeNuances.evidence: Rives J et al., Chirurgie 1973; Stoppa RE, World J Surg 1989

operativeNuances.nuance: For the TAR (Transversus Abdominis Release), after developing the retromuscular plane to the semilunar line, enter the plane between the internal oblique muscle and the transversus abdominis muscle. Identify the neurovascular bundles (they pass anterior to the transversus abdominis). Divide the transversus abdominis muscle 1-2 cm medial to the neurovascular bundles. This releases the lateral abdominal wall, gaining 4-8 cm of midline advancement per side.
operativeNuances.evidence: Novitsky YW et al., Am J Surg 2012; Rosen MJ et al., Surgery 2015

operativeNuances.nuance: During laparoscopic IPOM (Intraperitoneal Onlay Mesh) repair, ensure the mesh overlaps the defect by at least 5 cm in all directions. Use a composite mesh (anti-adhesive barrier on the visceral side, polypropylene on the parietal side). Fix the mesh with transfascial sutures at the four cardinal points PLUS double-crown tacks (inner and outer circle of tacks at 2 cm intervals).
operativeNuances.evidence: Heniford BT et al., Ann Surg 2000; Bittner R et al., Hernia 2014

operativeNuances.nuance: In the eTEP approach for midline incisional hernia, the key step is to establish the retromuscular space away from the hernia defect (lateral to the rectus sheath on one side). Enter the retromuscular plane, cross the midline behind the linea alba, and develop the space bilaterally. The entire dissection is done in the retromuscular plane — no entry into the peritoneal cavity (unless needed for adhesiolysis). This reduces ileus and adhesion formation.
operativeNuances.evidence: Daes J, Surg Endosc 2012; Belyansky I et al., Surg Endosc 2018

operativeNuances.nuance: When closing the fascia in component separation repairs, use a slowly absorbable monofilament suture (PDS loop) in a 'double-loop' technique: a far-near-near-far suture pattern that distributes tension evenly across the fascial closure. The suture-to-wound length ratio should be at least 4:1. Do NOT use rapidly absorbable sutures (Vicryl) for fascial closure in hernia repair — they lose strength before adequate healing occurs.
operativeNuances.evidence: Israelsson LA et al., Ann Surg 2000; STOPPIT Trial 2015

operativeNuances.nuance: In patients with loss of domain where primary fascial closure is not possible despite component separation, consider the use of a 'facio-dermal flap' (using the hernia sac as an autologous fascial substitute) or biologic mesh bridge. These have higher recurrence rates than synthetic mesh with fascial closure but are acceptable when primary closure cannot be achieved.
operativeNuances.evidence: Slater NJ et al., World J Surg 2014; synthetic vs biologic mesh trials

operativeNuances.nuance: For drainage after retromuscular mesh repair: place a 15-Fr Blake drain or similar closed-suction drain in the retromuscular space on each side. Exits through separate stab incisions in the lower abdomen. Secure drains with stay sutures. Document drain output daily. Remove when output <30 ml/24h for two consecutive days, usually 1-2 weeks postoperatively.
operativeNuances.evidence: Novitsky YW, Textbook of Hernia Surgery 2018; European Hernia Society Guidelines

operativeNuances.nuance: In the posterior component separation with TAR, the key step after developing the retromuscular plane is to identify the neurovascular bundles (T7-T12 intercostal neurovascular bundles and L1 iliohypogastric/ilioinguinal nerves) as they emerge from between the internal oblique and transversus abdominis muscles at the lateral border of the rectus sheath. The TAR incision is made 1-2 cm medial to these bundles, dividing the transversus abdominis muscle. This preserves the innervation of the rectus muscle and the anterior abdominal wall sensation.
operativeNuances.evidence: Novitsky YW et al., Am J Surg 2012; Rosen MJ et al., Surgery 2015

operativeNuances.nuance: For contaminated incisional hernia repair (CDC Class III-IV), the strategy should be: (1) thorough debridement of all infected or necrotic tissue, (2) primary fascial closure if possible (tension-free), (3) placement of a slowly absorbable synthetic mesh (e.g., biosynthetic mesh like Phasix or TIGR Matrix) or biologic mesh if synthetic is absolutely contraindicated, (4) copious irrigation with warm saline, (5) placement of closed-suction drains in the wound, (6) delayed primary skin closure or healing by secondary intention. A two-stage approach (repair + delayed mesh at 6-12 months) is the safest alternative when there is uncertainty about infection clearance.
operativeNuances.evidence: Bittner R et al., Hernia 2021; modified WSES guidelines

prognosis.naturalHistory: Untreated incisional hernia slowly but progressively enlarges over years. Small defects (<4 cm) that are asymptomatic may remain stable for years, but most gradually increase in size due to persistent intra-abdominal pressure. Approximately 10-15% will eventually incarcerate, and 5-10% of those will strangulate. The annual risk of incarceration is approximately 2-4% per year. Loss of domain develops over 5-10 years in large, untreated hernias.
prognosis.recurrenceRates: After sublay (retromuscular) mesh repair with fascial closure: 4-8% at 5 years. After onlay mesh repair: 10-15% at 5 years. After IPOM (laparoscopic intraperitoneal mesh): 5-10% at 5 years. After component separation with TAR: 5-10% at 3 years. After primary repair without mesh: 30-50% at 2 years. After repair in contaminated fields: 20-40% recurrence. Recurrence rates are higher in obese, smokers, and immunosuppressed patients.
prognosis.cancerRisk: Incisional hernia itself does not increase cancer risk. However, large incisional hernias containing colon may rarely be associated with incarceration of a colonic neoplasm. The primary cancer concern is for the original indication for laparotomy (e.g., colorectal cancer) — appropriate cancer surveillance should continue regardless of hernia status.
prognosis.qualityOfLife: Untreated incisional hernia significantly impairs quality of life due to pain, discomfort, cosmetic concerns, and activity limitation. After successful repair with mesh, quality of life improves dramatically in most domains. Chronic pain (5-10% incidence) is the main factor limiting full recovery. Mesh sensation (foreign body feeling) affects up to 20% of patients but usually resolves within 6-12 months. Return to heavy physical labour is possible in 70-80% of patients after full recovery.
prognosis.mortality: Elective incisional hernia repair: mortality <0.5% for W1-W2 defects, 1-2% for complex W3 defects. Emergency repair for strangulation: 5-10% mortality. Emergency repair with bowel resection: 10-15% mortality. Loss of domain repair with pneumoperitoneum or Botox preparation: <1% mortality in elective settings. Mortality is primarily from perioperative cardiovascular events, pulmonary complications, and sepsis in emergency cases.
prognosis.diseaseModifyingFactors: Factors associated with worse prognosis: obesity (BMI >35), smoking, diabetes, immunosuppression, COPD with chronic cough, wound infection at primary surgery, multiple prior repairs, defect >10 cm (W3), and loss of domain >20%. Protective factors: healthy weight, non-smoker, primary (first-time) repair, defect <4 cm, sublay mesh placement, fascial closure with component separation, and elective (non-emergency) repair.
prognosis.biomechanicalPrinciplesIncisional: The success of incisional hernia repair depends on restoring the dynamic function of the abdominal wall: (1) the 'keystone' concept — the linea alba is the central tendon of the abdominal wall, and its repair restores the normal vector forces of the rectus and oblique muscles, (2) the mesh must be placed in the plane that provides the best mechanical advantage — retromuscular (sublay) placement harnesses the intra-abdominal pressure to press the mesh against the posterior rectus sheath, actually strengthening the repair with increased pressure (Pascal's principle), while onlay placement can be lifted off the fascia by subcutaneous fluid collections, and (3) the mesh must be large enough to distribute the stress from the defect across a wide area — a 'stress shielding' effect that protects the native fascial edges from the high stress concentrations that would otherwise lead to suture pull-through and recurrence.
prognosis.molecularWoundHealing: The fascial healing process after incisional hernia repair depends on: (1) adequate fibroblast recruitment and activation by TGF-β1 released from platelets and macrophages in the wound, (2) balanced collagen synthesis (Type I for strength, Type III for early scaffolding) with a gradual shift toward Type I dominance over 6-12 months, (3) appropriate angiogenesis driven by VEGF to supply the metabolic demands of the healing tissue, (4) cross-linking of collagen fibrils by lysyl oxidase (LOX) to achieve mechanical strength, which is copper-dependent and impaired in malnourished patients, and (5) gradual remodeling of the scar over 12-24 months, during which the tensile strength increases from 30% of normal fascia at 3 months to 70-80% at 12 months.

presentingComplaints: A bulge or lump at or near a previous surgical scar
The lump appears when standing, coughing, or straining
The lump disappears when lying down
Dragging sensation or discomfort at the hernia site, especially at the end of the day
The hernia has been gradually increasing in size over months or years

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Incarcerated/strangulated/loss of domain -> SEVERE; reducible small-medium -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: When did you have your last abdominal surgery and what was it for?
Did your surgical wound get infected after the operation?
When did you first notice a bulge at the scar?
Does the bulge go away when you lie down?
Can you push the bulge back in yourself?
Has the hernia been getting bigger over time?
Do you have any pain or discomfort at the hernia site?
Do you have any difficulty passing stool or gas?
Have you had any episodes where the hernia got stuck and painful?
Do you have nausea or vomiting?
How many previous hernia repairs have you had?
Do you smoke or have a chronic cough?
Are you overweight or obese?
Do you have diabetes, kidney disease, or any chronic illness?
Do you take steroids or any immunosuppressant medication?
Have you had any abdominal infections or wound problems after surgery?
Do you do any heavy lifting or strenuous work?
Have you ever had a CT scan of the hernia?
What is your nutritional status — have you lost weight recently?
Are you planning to have more children (if female)?
Have you had any previous hernia repairs using mesh?
Do you wear an abdominal binder or support belt?
Has the hernia ever been stuck and required emergency treatment?
What is your daily occupation — do you do farming, heavy lifting, or long-distance driving (boda-boda)?
Do you have any chronic liver disease, TB, or HIV?
Have you had any radiotherapy to the abdomen?
Do you take steroid medications or any immunosuppressants?
Have you ever had Botox injections or progressive pneumoperitoneum to prepare for hernia surgery?
Do you wear an abdominal binder regularly? Does it help?
How many previous laparotomies have you had through the same incision?
Did you have a drain placed after your previous surgery? Any complications?
Do you have any pain at the hernia site that prevents you from working?
Have you ever been told you have a 'loss of domain' or that the hernia is too large for simple repair?
Do you have any skin changes over the hernia — thinning, redness, or ulceration?
Have you had any previous wound infections that required opening of the wound?
Do you have any difficulty with breathing when lying flat (suggesting loss of domain with respiratory compromise)?
Have you had any previous hernia repairs using mesh? Do you know what type of mesh was used?
Do you have any chronic pain or discomfort at the hernia site that affects your sleep?
Have you ever been advised to lose weight before hernia surgery? How much weight have you lost?
Do you have a stoma (colostomy or ileostomy) that is located near or within the hernia?
Have you had any investigations like CT scan or ultrasound for this hernia?
Do you have a family history of hernias, aneurysms, or connective tissue disorders?

examination_findings.finding: Palpable bulge at or adjacent to a surgical scar — most commonly midline
Cough impulse — the bulge expands with coughing
Palpable fascial defect — measure width and length
Reducible vs irreducible — assess reducibility
Irreducibility and tenderness suggesting incarceration
Overlying skin thinning, ulceration, or ischemic changes
Signs of bowel obstruction — distension, absent bowel sounds
Loss of domain — the hernia contents cannot be completely reduced into the abdomen
Multiple defects — may be Swiss cheese pattern
Widened diastasis of rectus muscles
Fever and tachycardia in strangulation
Bowel sounds auscultable over the hernia
Obvious skin changes — excoriation, ulceration, infection
Palpable omentum or bowel loops in the sac
Loss of abdominal wall compliance — the muscles feel tight and retracted laterally
In thin patients, the edges of the fascial defect can be clearly outlined as a firm ring
Cough impulse may be absent in large hernias with loss of domain (the sac does not expand further)
Previous mesh may be palpable as a firm plaque beneath the scar
With the patient standing, the hernia becomes more prominent and the defect may gape open
Measurement of the distance between the xiphisternum and pubic symphysis — useful for preoperative planning and assessing tension on closure
Palpation of the lateral abdominal wall muscle bulk and tension — thin, contracted muscles suggest chronic loss of domain and need for component separation

The 'dive sign': when the patient performs a sit-up from supine, the hernia contents visibly withdraw into the abdomen as the rectus muscles contract, confirming that the defect is within the anterior abdominal wall
The hernia may be partially reducible with a 'gurgle' (suggesting bowel) or 'silent' (suggesting omentum or omentum adherent to the sac)
In thin patients, the fascial edges of the defect can be clearly outlined with the examining fingertip, measuring the defect dimensions precisely
Multiple separate fascial defects ('Swiss cheese') may be palpable as distinct holes in the linea alba
Cough impulse may be absent in large hernias with loss of domain because the hernia contents fill the sac completely and do not expand further with coughing
Rectus diastasis co-exists in 30-40% of patients with incisional hernia — measure the inter-rectus distance above and below the umbilicus

bedside_scores.name: European Hernia Society (EHS) Classification for Incisional Hernia

components.name: Recurrence: R0 primary, R1 first recurrence, R+ multiple

bedside_scores.interpretation: Used for standardization and surgical planning. W1: small, W2: medium, W3: large. Sublay mesh recommended for all.

riskFactors.note: Strongest risk factor — 40% of infected wounds develop hernia
Higher tension closure, less attention to technique
Midline heals with lower tensile strength

subtypes.name: Small Incisional Hernia (<4cm / EHS W1)
subtypes.management: Elective repair with mesh. Onlay mesh (suprafascial) for small defects. Sublay (retromuscular) mesh preferred. Primary closure possible only for very small defects (<2cm). Open or laparoscopic IPOM (intraperitoneal onlay mesh).

subtypes.name: Large/Complex Incisional Hernia (>=4cm or W2-W3)
subtypes.management: Pre-operative optimization: weight loss, smoking cessation, diabetes control. Botulinum toxin A injections into lateral abdominal muscles (2-3 weeks before surgery). Progressive pneumoperitoneum for loss of domain. Surgical: Component separation (anterior rectus release or posterior component separation/TAR) with mesh reinforcement (sublay or underlay preferred). Open repair with retromuscular mesh (Rives-Stoppa). Minimally invasive: IPOM, eTEP (extended totally extraperitoneal). Emergency surgery if incarcerated/strangulated.

subtypes.name: Port-Site Incisional Hernia
subtypes.management: Elective primary suture repair if defect <1 cm. Mesh repair (onlay or sublay) if defect >1 cm or recurrent. Laparoscopic IPOM is an option. Higher risk of incarceration despite small size because the defect is a rigid ring in the fascia.

subtypes.name: Recurrent Incisional Hernia (After Previous Mesh Repair)
subtypes.management: Complex surgical planning: CT to map defect relative to existing mesh. Options: (a) Laparoscopic IPOM over the previous mesh if the mesh is intact; (b) Open sublay with new mesh placed over or behind the old mesh; (c) Component separation with TAR for large recurrences. Consider removing old mesh if infected. Higher recurrence rate (15-25%).

bedside.name: Palpation of fascial defect with patient supine and standing
bedside.indication: measure defect size, reducibility, and assess for multiple defects

imaging.name: Abdominal Ultrasound
imaging.indication: assess defect size, contents, vascularity with Doppler

imaging.name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis with Valsalva
imaging.indication: gold standard — defect measurement, loss of domain calculation, contents, associated pathology

imaging.name: MRI Abdomen
imaging.indication: alternative if CT contraindicated (pregnancy, contrast allergy), better for soft tissue assessment

interpretation_rules.ct_with_valsalva: CT with Valsalva maneuver maximally demonstrates hernia contents and allows accurate measurement of defect and loss of domain. 'Loss of domain' = ratio of hernia sac volume to abdominal cavity volume >20%.
interpretation_rules.defect_width_surgery: Defect width determines surgical approach: W1 (<4cm): may close primarily with mesh. W2 (4-10cm): component separation likely needed. W3 (>10cm): complex reconstruction with component separation ± TAR.
interpretation_rules.preop_optimization: Surgery should be delayed 4-6 weeks for: smoking cessation, weight loss target BMI <35, HbA1c <7%, albumin >3.0 g/dL. Botox injections 2-4 weeks before surgery for large defects.
interpretation_rules.mesh_choice_surgery: Sublay (retromuscular) mesh has the lowest recurrence rate. Onlay is simpler but higher seroma rate. IPOM is laparoscopic but higher cost and adhesions.

management.protocolReference: International Endohernia Society (IEHS) Guidelines for Incisional Hernia (2023)
management.initial: CT scan for defect mapping and loss of domain assessment. Pre-operative optimization: smoking cessation (minimum 4 weeks), weight loss (target BMI <35), HbA1c <7%, albumin >3.0 g/dL. Prehabilitation exercises. Botulinum toxin A injections for large defects (300-500 units total, 10-15 injections into lateral abdominal muscles). Progressive pneumoperitoneum (5-14 days insufflation) for loss of domain >20%.
management.definitive: Open repair: Retromuscular (Rives-Stoppa) sublay mesh repair — gold standard for midline hernias. Component separation (anterior rectus release or posterior component separation with TAR) for large defects >10cm. Laparoscopic: IPOM (intraperitoneal onlay mesh) — preferred for small-medium defects not requiring component separation. eTEP (extended TEP) — newer technique for minimally invasive retromuscular repair. Emergency repair: for incarcerated/strangulated — open or laparoscopic reduction ± bowel resection. Mesh repair if clean; primary repair + delayed mesh if contaminated.
management.postop: Wound care, drain management. VTE prophylaxis (LMWH for 4 weeks). Abdominal binder for comfort and support for 6 weeks. No heavy lifting (>5kg) for 6-8 weeks. Return to desk work 2-4 weeks, manual labour 6-12 weeks. Wound infection and seroma are common — manage proactively.

pathways.criteria: defect <10cm (W1-W2), no loss of domain, reducible
pathways.treatment: Open sublay mesh (Rives-Stoppa) or laparoscopic IPOM/eTEP. Primary fascial closure if possible. Preoperative optimization as needed.

pathways.criteria: defect >=10cm (W3), loss of domain, multiple recurrences
pathways.treatment: Pre-operative optimization: weight loss, smoking cessation, diabetes control, Botox injections. Component separation with TAR. Retromuscular mesh. Pneumoperitoneum if loss of domain >20%.
Emergency surgery (open or laparoscopic). Reduce contents, assess bowel viability. Resect if non-viable. Mesh repair if clean. Primary repair if contaminated. Damage control surgery if unstable.

operative_templates.indication: Primary incisional hernia (midline) with defect width 4-10cm
operative_templates.incision: Elliptical excision of the old scar with extension above and below the defect
steps: Elliptical excision of old scar, dissect subcutaneous flaps
Identify the anterior rectus sheath and the hernia defect
Open the anterior rectus sheath longitudinally 1cm from the medial border
Dissect the rectus muscle off the posterior rectus sheath (retromuscular plane)
Continue dissection laterally to the semilunar line bilaterally
Close the posterior rectus sheath and peritoneum in the midline — reconstruct the sac
Place a large mesh (at least 5cm overlap beyond the defect in all directions) in the retromuscular space
Secure the mesh with transfascial sutures or absorbable tackers (if using self-gripping mesh)
Close the anterior rectus sheath in the midline with slowly absorbable suture (size 1 loop)
Place drains in the retromuscular space (exits through the skin)
Close subcutaneous tissue and skin

operative_templates.procedure: Posterior Component Separation with TAR (Transversus Abdominis Release)
operative_templates.indication: Large incisional hernia >10cm with loss of domain or recurrence after prior repair
operative_templates.incision: Midline laparotomy with excision of old scar
steps: Midline approach with excision of old scar
Develop retromuscular plane bilaterally as in Rives-Stoppa
Identify the neurovascular bundles (T7-T12, L1) at the lateral edge of the rectus sheath
Enter the plane between the internal oblique and transversus abdominis muscles 1cm medial to the neurovascular bundles
Divide the transversus abdominis muscle lateral to the neurovascular bundles — TAR release
Continue the dissection laterally into the preperitoneal space as needed
Close the posterior layer (peritoneum + posterior rectus sheath) in the midline
Place a large mesh (at least 5cm overlap) in the retromuscular space
Close the anterior rectus sheath fascia in the midline
Place drains, close skin

dischargeCriteria: Drains in situ with decreasing output
Wound clean and dry (or minor concerns manageable at home)
No signs of infection (afebrile, CRP trending down)
Drain and wound care teaching completed

patient_education.explanation: An incisional hernia is a bulge that develops at the site of a previous surgical scar. It happens when the muscle layer (fascia) does not heal properly and a gap forms. In Kenya, this is very common after emergency operations for trauma, peritonitis, or Caesarean section, especially when the wound became infected. The hernia will gradually get bigger over time. Surgery is needed to close the gap, usually with a mesh (a synthetic patch) to reinforce the weak area. Complex hernias need special preparation before surgery, including weight loss and sometimes Botox injections to relax the muscles.
patient_education.warningSigns: Return to hospital immediately if you have: the hernia becomes hard, tender, and cannot be pushed back in, you develop nausea, vomiting, or abdominal swelling, the skin over the hernia turns red or dusky, you have fever with chills, or you have wound discharge or separation after surgery.
patient_education.activity: After surgery, use an abdominal binder for support for 6 weeks. No heavy lifting (>5kg) for 6-8 weeks. Walking is encouraged from day 1. Return to desk work in 2-4 weeks. Return to manual labour, farming, or boda-boda riding: 8-12 weeks. Avoid contact sports for 3 months. Do not drive for at least 2 weeks.
patient_education.diet: Eat a high-fiber diet to prevent constipation — fruits, vegetables, whole grains. Drink 8-10 glasses of water daily. Use stool softeners if needed. Avoid overeating — small frequent meals are better. Achieve and maintain a healthy weight — this is one of the most important things you can do to prevent recurrence.
patient_education.medication: Take all prescribed medications as directed. Continue smoking cessation (this is essential for healing). You may be on antibiotics if drains are in place. Use stool softeners for at least 2 weeks. If you are diabetic, maintain good blood sugar control.
patient_education.followUp: Drains will be removed in 1-3 weeks depending on output. You will be seen at 2 weeks for wound review, 6 weeks for recovery assessment, and 6 months and 1 year to check for recurrence. If you had a mesh repair, you need to inform future doctors about the mesh.

followup.before_discharge: Drain care teaching. Abdominal binder fitting. Wound assessment. Activity restriction counseling. Ensure adequate pain control.

after_discharge.1_week: Wound check, drain removal if output <30ml/day. Assess for seroma, infection. Pain control review.
after_discharge.2_weeks: Wound review, suture removal. Assess for recurrence early. Review drain site healing. Discuss returning to light activity.
after_discharge.6_weeks: Clinical assessment, check for recurrence. Full activity release if healing complete. Discuss long-term weight management and lifestyle modifications.
after_discharge.3_months: Check for recurrence, chronic pain assessment. Surveillance CT if complex repair.
after_discharge.6_months: Long-term follow-up to confirm no recurrence. Discharge to primary care with instructions for self-examination.

followup.when_to_return: Immediately if: sudden increase in pain, fever, wound discharge or redness, inability to pass urine, or hernia becomes irreducible.
